core_intuitions: The shared-prefix problem: real traffic (system prompts, few-shot blocks, agent forks, multi-turn chat) re-prefills the SAME leading tokens over and over; RadixAttention's promise is to compute each prefix's KV at most once and reuse it for any prompt that starts with it.

A radix tree is a compressed prefix trie over TOKEN IDs where each node owns the KV-cache SLOT INDICES (not tensors) for its edge — so one paged KV block can be referenced by many nodes, and the tree is a thin index layer on top of PagedAttention.

The insert path is match -> (on partial overlap) SPLIT the edge into a shared node + remainder -> attach the divergent suffix as a new leaf; no KV is recomputed on a split, only index tensors are re-sliced.

Eviction is LRU restricted to unlocked LEAVES: you peel cold tips inward so you never orphan a live prefix, lock_ref pinning protects KV a running kernel is reading, and freeing a leaf can turn its parent into a newly-evictable leaf (a cascade).

The zero-overhead / overlap scheduler runs the CPU's batch preparation for step t+1 UNDER the GPU's forward pass for step t, removing the scheduling bubble that otherwise idles the GPU every decode step.
The frontend (gen/select/fork/join) turns prefix sharing from a lucky cache hit into a PLANNED, co-scheduled execution: fork(k) statically guarantees k branches share the parent's KV path.

existing.name: radix-tree
existing.issue: The core idea (shared system-prompt edge, branch into task templates, diverge into unique leaves, KV computed once) reads clearly and correctly in both themes. Two small cosmetic defects: the 'root' text label overflows its 13px-radius circle so it slightly clips at the node edges, and the right-side dashed annotation ('one edge holds the whole shared prompt...') sits close enough to the shared-prefix box that the dashed leader line and text crowd the box's right border.
existing.improvement: Enlarge the root node slightly (or shrink/relabel the 'root' text so it fits inside the circle without clipping), and nudge the right-side annotation block a bit further right / shorten its leader line so it no longer crowds the shared-prefix box. Optionally add a one-line reinforcement to the caption that matching walks root->longest-cached-prefix and only the divergent suffix is recomputed (already implied; keep it). No structural change — the figure's content and both-theme rendering are otherwise good.

new_figures.name: radix-match-split-insert
new_figures.concept: The dynamic insert path for one arriving request: walk the tree matching full edges, hit an edge that agrees only partway, SPLIT it into a shared node + remainder, then attach the divergent suffix as a fresh leaf — with KV slot indices re-sliced, never recomputed.
new_figures.rationale: The static radix-tree figure shows an end-state but not the OPERATION that is the conceptual crux of the whole chapter and the from-scratch section: match -> split -> insert. The split-on-partial-match is the single trickiest mechanic (the text spends a full subsection and a before/after ASCII on it), and it is genuinely a multi-step process — a new key streaming down, a mid-edge divergence, an edge cleaving in two, a new leaf attaching. Motion makes 'no KV is recomputed, we only re-slice index tensors' physical. Nothing currently animates this.
new_figures.placement: Splitting and inserting (after the before/after ASCII split diagram)
new_figures.brief:
A small radix tree, drawn top-down, that transforms as a new request is inserted. Use short illustrative token strings shown as characters standing in for token IDs, and show each edge carrying a little strip of KV SLOT INDEX boxes (small integers) to reinforce that nodes own indices, not tensors.

Initial state (must stand alone as a static frame): root -> one edge labelled with a system-prompt string, e.g. "You are a helpful assistant. Be concise.", whose edge shows KV slots [0..7] (use small illustrative integers). That edge ends at node A (a leaf). Off to the side, an incoming request card shows its prompt: "You are a helpful assistant. " + "Summarize:" — i.e. it shares only the first part of A's edge.

Stage 1 MATCH: highlight the incoming key walking down from root along A's edge; a small caret/marker advances token-by-token along the edge while a running 'matched = k' counter ticks up. It stops where the strings diverge (after 'assistant. '), showing 'partial match: agrees for prefix_len tokens then diverges'.

Stage 2 SPLIT: the single edge visibly CLEAVES at the divergence point into two: a new intermediate node S owning the shared prefix and its KV slots [0..4] (the shared slice), and the original node A re-parented under S carrying the remainder 'Be concise.' with the REMAINING slots [5..7]. Annotate emphatically: 'no KV recomputed — index tensor is re-sliced [0..4] | [5..7]'. Keep A's box tagged 'original KV preserved'.

Stage 3 INSERT: the request's divergent suffix 'Summarize:' attaches as a NEW leaf child of S, and it gets FRESH KV slots (new integers, e.g. [8..10]) drawn in a visually distinct 'newly allocated' style. A badge summarizes: 'reused = shared prefix; prefilled = suffix only'.

Animation sequencing: match (caret travels) -> pause at divergence -> split (edge cleaves, S appears, A slides down under S) -> insert (new leaf grows from S) -> brief hold, then loop. The static end-frame should legibly show the final S/A/new-leaf tree with all three slot strips.

Theme-safe: neutral node/edge strokes; use ONE accent for the shared/reused KV and a distinct second accent (e.g. the warn token) ONLY for the freshly-allocated suffix slots, so 'reused vs newly prefilled' is a two-color story. No overlapping labels; route the re-parenting cleanly. Everything inside the viewBox.

new_figures.name: radix-lru-eviction
new_figures.concept: LRU eviction restricted to unlocked leaves: hot recently-used prefixes are pinned (lock_ref>0) and survive, cold leaves are popped oldest-first from a min-heap by last-access and freed from the tips inward, and freeing a leaf can promote its now-childless parent into a newly-evictable leaf.
new_figures.rationale: Eviction is one of the four named core operations and a distinct intuition the chapter stresses (leaf-only, lock-aware, cache-aware order) yet has NO visual. The subtle, foundational parts — why you evict from the tips inward (never orphan a prefix), why locked nodes are skipped, and the CASCADE where a freed leaf turns its parent into the next evictable leaf — are exactly the things a static tree cannot convey but a short animated peel makes obvious. Serves the 'Eviction: LRU over leaves' section and the interview answer on bounding memory.
new_figures.placement: Eviction: LRU over leaves (after the evict() code block and the three-properties list)
new_figures.brief:
Left: a radix tree with a clearly HOT shared prefix near the root (a wide edge labelled 'system prompt', tagged with a lock icon and 'lock_ref>0 · pinned') from which several branches descend to leaves. Give each leaf a small 'last-access' timestamp badge using small illustrative integers (e.g. t=9 fresh, t=2 old, t=1 oldest) and mark one branch's leaf with a lock icon ('a running request holds this path'). Right: a compact vertical 'min-heap by last_access (oldest on top)' showing the same leaves ordered by their timestamp.

Static frame (stands alone): shows the tree, the lock badges, the timestamps, and the heap, with a one-line legend: 'evict oldest, unlocked, LEAF nodes first; pinned nodes are skipped; hot prefix survives'. An arrow labelled 'free KV -> paged pool' points from the heap top back toward the KV pool.

Animation (the payoff): (1) pop the oldest unlocked leaf off the heap, fade/remove it from the tree, and animate its KV slots returning to the pool ('free(node.value)'); (2) show a LOCKED leaf reached next being SKIPPED (a small 'skip: lock_ref>0' tag, it stays); (3) after the last child of an interior node is freed, that parent PULSES and gets re-tagged 'now a leaf -> evictable' and is pushed onto the heap — the cascade; (4) the pinned hot system-prompt prefix visibly REMAINS throughout. Loop after a short hold on the peeled-back tree.

Make the reading crystal: eviction proceeds from the TIPS inward, never touching an interior node with live children, never touching a locked node, and the hot prefix at the root is what's left standing. Theme-safe: neutral strokes; one accent for 'freed/returning KV', a distinct treatment (e.g. muted + lock glyph) for pinned/surviving nodes. Keep timestamps and the heap column legible; no overlaps; all within the viewBox.

new_figures.name: overlap-scheduler-timeline
new_figures.concept: A Gantt-style comparison of the decode loop without vs with overlap scheduling: without overlap the CPU and GPU alternate so the GPU idles during each scheduling gap; with overlap the CPU prepares step t+1 UNDER the GPU's forward for step t, so GPU kernels launch back-to-back with no bubble.
new_figures.rationale: The zero-overhead scheduler is a headline SGLang contribution and a distinct, foundational throughput intuition ('the CPU scheduler is in the critical path; idling the GPU is the cardinal sin'). The chapter conveys it only with a crude ASCII timeline; a clean two-row Gantt makes the removed SERIAL DEPENDENCY and the reclaimed GPU idle time immediately legible. This is inherently a timeline comparison — a crisp static infographic captures it fully (motion not required).
new_figures.placement: The "zero-overhead" scheduler (replacing/upgrading the ASCII 'Without overlap / With overlap' timeline block)
new_figures.brief:
Two stacked Gantt-style timeline panels sharing a common left-to-right time axis, each panel with two lanes: a 'CPU (scheduling)' lane and a 'GPU (forward pass)' lane. Use small illustrative durations only (e.g. depict a GPU forward block as roughly twice the width of a CPU scheduling block) — invent NO real millisecond benchmark numbers; if you must annotate, use generic tokens like 'fwd t' and 'sched t+1'.

Top panel — 'Without overlap (serial)': CPU lane shows blocks [sched t][sched t+1][sched t+2] and GPU lane shows [fwd t][fwd t+1][fwd t+2], but the GPU blocks are pushed right so each starts only AFTER its sched block finishes. Draw hatched/greyed 'IDLE' gaps in the GPU lane between forward blocks, with a small bracket labelled 'GPU idle: waiting on the CPU'. A side tag: 'CPU is in the critical path'.

Bottom panel — 'With overlap scheduling': CPU lane shows sched blocks packed contiguously [sched t][sched t+1][sched t+2][sched t+3]; GPU lane shows [fwd t][fwd t+1][fwd t+2] BACK-TO-BACK with NO gaps. Draw thin vertical alignment guides showing that 'sched t+1' sits directly UNDER 'fwd t' (prepared while the GPU runs the prior step). A side tag: 'CPU step t+1 runs under GPU step t -> no bubble'.

Between or beside the panels add a short takeaway strip: 'overlap removes a serial dependency -> the decode loop becomes near-pure GPU work (compounds with CUDA graphs)'. Optionally a tiny paired 'GPU utilization' meter beside each panel (partial bar top, near-full bar bottom) using only illustrative proportions.

Theme-safe: neutral lane backgrounds; use ONE accent to fill the GPU forward blocks (the resource that matters), a muted hatch for CPU blocks, and a clearly-hatched neutral for the IDLE gaps so the top panel visibly 'leaks' time and the bottom does not. Align both panels on the same time origin so the eye compares total makespan. No overlapping labels; everything inside the viewBox; legible in both themes.

summary: The chapter is concept-dense and currently served by a single strong static tree figure (radix-tree), which I keep with minor refinements (a clipped 'root' label and a crowded side annotation). Three foundational mechanisms remain text/ASCII-only: the insert path (match->split->insert), LRU-over-leaves eviction with lock pinning, and the overlap scheduler. I propose (1) a long-animation of match/split/insert — the chapter's conceptual crux, where motion makes 're-slice indices, don't recompute KV' physical; (2) a short animation of LRU eviction that dramatizes peeling cold tips inward, skipping locked nodes, and the parent-becomes-leaf cascade; and (3) a crisp static Gantt comparison of the zero-overhead overlap scheduler. All briefs are theme-safe, use only small illustrative integers, and invent no benchmark numbers; the two animations each stand alone as a static end-frame.
